assessmentProfile.inLessonPolicy: Use concise concept checks; coding and hybrid topics include at least one code-reading check.
assessmentProfile.label: Mental model plus practical code reasoning
assessmentProfile.rationale: The topic combines an important model with implementation consequences, so both explanation and code reasoning are required.
assessmentProfile.topicQuizPolicy: Questions Q04–Q08 form the topic quiz. The presentation mix follows this profile.

commonMistakes: Memorizing syntax without predicting the underlying value or execution order.
Mutating shared data when the task expects a new value or explicit ownership.
Treating browser APIs and language features as the same layer.
Choosing an abstraction from habit rather than required operations.

contentAuthority.canonical: New JS2Next Release 1 curriculum and independently authored content.
contentAuthority.courseTranscripts: Coverage and sequencing research only; never learner-facing authority.
contentAuthority.legacyProjectContent: Supplementary internal reference only; excluded from runtime unless explicitly re-authored and approved.

learningObjectives.text: Explain arrays and objects using a new example and justify the result.
Explain sets and maps using a new example and justify the result.
Choose and justify selection criteria and trade-offs using a new example and justify the result.

mentalModel: Draw a decision map: **Arrays and objects** → **Sets and maps** → **Selection criteria and trade-offs**. Add branches where the learner must choose an alternative and label the evidence for the choice.

choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Choosing the Right Data Structure rule and the observable result.
choices.label: Choose a data structure by the dominant operations: ordered iteration, lookup, uniqueness, insertion, deletion, and serialization. No structure is universally best.

choices.feedback: A Set models unique values and membership. A Map models an explicit key-value collection with flexible keys and predictable iteration.

choices.feedback: An array stores order, a Map provides keyed lookup, and a Set enforces uniqueness.
choices.label: An array stores order, a Map provides keyed lookup, and a Set enforces uniqueness.

choices.label: The code works only because Choosing the Right Data Structure removes the need to track inputs or state.

questions.prompt: A developer encounters this code during a review. Which explanation best describes its relevant Choosing the Right Data Structure behavior?

choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Choosing the Right Data Structure rule and the observable result.
choices.label: Objects cannot store any keys.

choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Choosing the Right Data Structure rule and the observable result.
choices.label: The values are combined into an array automatically.

choices.feedback: Plain object keys are strings or symbols, so both object keys coerce to the same string and the later assignment overwrites the earlier one.
choices.label: Plain object keys are strings or symbols, so both object keys coerce to the same string and the later assignment overwrites the earlier one.

choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Choosing the Right Data Structure rule and the observable result.
choices.label: The first assignment makes `userA` immutable.

questions.explanation: Plain object keys are strings or symbols, so both object keys coerce to the same string and the later assignment overwrites the earlier one.
questions.hint: Trace the actual values, references, call form, render cycle, request flow, or server/client boundary before choosing.
questions.prompt: Why can this lookup return the wrong permission?

choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Choosing the Right Data Structure rule and the observable result.
choices.label: Serialize both objects with the same constant key.

choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Choosing the Right Data Structure rule and the observable result.
choices.label: Use an array and search by object string conversion.

choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Choosing the Right Data Structure rule and the observable result.
choices.label: Add more properties to `permissions` without changing the key model.

choices.feedback: Use a `Map` when object identity is the key: `permissions.set(userA, 'editor')` and `permissions.set(userB, 'viewer')`.
choices.label: Use a `Map` when object identity is the key: `permissions.set(userA, 'editor')` and `permissions.set(userB, 'viewer')`.

questions.explanation: Use a `Map` when object identity is the key: `permissions.set(userA, 'editor')` and `permissions.set(userB, 'viewer')`.
questions.hint: Choose the smallest change that fixes the underlying model, not a workaround that hides the symptom.
questions.prompt: Which change is the best fix?

choices.feedback: Use the Choosing the Right Data Structure mental model to explain the code's behavior, then choose the smallest implementation whose boundary and trade-offs match the requirement.
choices.label: Use the Choosing the Right Data Structure mental model to explain the code's behavior, then choose the smallest implementation whose boundary and trade-offs match the requirement.

choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Choosing the Right Data Structure rule and the observable result.
choices.label: Use Choosing the Right Data Structure in every file because more abstraction always improves software.

choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Choosing the Right Data Structure rule and the observable result.
choices.label: Avoid Choosing the Right Data Structure completely because every additional concept reduces maintainability.

choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Choosing the Right Data Structure rule and the observable result.
choices.label: Choose only by the number of lines and ignore correctness, ownership, and future change.

questions.explanation: Use the Choosing the Right Data Structure mental model to explain the code's behavior, then choose the smallest implementation whose boundary and trade-offs match the requirement.
questions.hint: Choose the option that balances correctness, clarity, cost, and the actual requirement.
questions.prompt: Which guideline shows the best engineering judgment about Choosing the Right Data Structure?

summary: Choosing the Right Data Structure connects Arrays and objects, Sets and maps, Selection criteria and trade-offs.
Use the topic to make behavior and decisions explicit rather than memorizing isolated syntax.
Verify understanding by explaining a new example and answering the topic quiz without looking back.
title: Choosing the Right Data Structure

topicQuiz.failurePolicy: Show explanations, add missed objectives to local review, and allow immediate or later retry.

whyThisMatters: Learners frequently use choosing the right data structure without a clear model of why it behaves as it does; this lesson makes that model explicit. It belongs to the **Data Structures and Functional Techniques** module.
